library.title: Top 50 Landmine Exercise Library
library.purpose: Build a coachable landmine exercise library covering one-handed, two-handed, ball-grip, and handle-grip variations for strength, power, trunk control, pulling, lower-body loading, and grip-specific athletic transfer.
library.authoringRule: Use camelCase Card v2 shape: identity fields, primaryPhaseKey/subrole/slot, taxonomy tags with key + weight, movementRequirements, coachingExecution, dosage, scaling cohorts, safety, regimen, and phaseProfile. Landmine cards are not HIIT by default; keep them strength, power, resilience, or control focused unless explicitly re-authored.
sourceBasis: Purpose.txt status: a standalone Purpose.txt was not located in the accessible File Library search results, so this deliverable does not pretend Purpose.txt was read or cite it as authority.

Card v2 basis: accessible Vortex exercise-card files in the File Library repeatedly describe exercise_card_details_for_llm.md as the current authoring guide for phase-first authoring, canonical primaryPhaseKey/subrole/slot, taxonomy key + weight tags, publish gates, movementRequirements, coachingExecution, dosage, scaling cohorts, safety, pairing logic, regimen, phaseProfile, and anti-patterns.

Landmine precedent: existing Vortex card examples surfaced in the File Library include Landmine Press and Landmine Rotational Press as loaded strength/power-strength patterns; this library extends that logic to one-handed, two-handed, ball-grip, and handle-grip landmine variations.

Coaching basis: exercise selection prioritizes coachable landmine patterns that provide a stable angled bar path, scalable loading, rotational transfer, trunk bracing, grip options, and manageable shoulder demands while avoiding fatigue-chasing misuse.
library.sourceCaveat: Purpose.txt was requested but not located in accessible File Library search results; exercise_card_details_for_llm.md was applied through the Card v2 requirements repeatedly summarized in accessible Vortex files.
library.programmingRule: Landmine work is quality-first. Pressing, rowing, squatting, hinging, and rotating should stay repeatable. Output variations require freshness, low volume, and full recovery; Capacity variations use progressive load without conditioning drift; Resilience variations use bracing and control without threat or fatigue chasing.

categories.title: One-Handed Pressing, Stance, and Shoulder-Friendly Push Strength
categories.focus: One-arm angled pressing patterns that teach scapular upward rotation, trunk bracing, rib control, and unilateral force transfer without requiring a straight overhead bar path.

categories.title: Two-Handed Pressing, Squat, and Lower-Body Strength
categories.focus: Two-hand landmine patterns that create a clear loading problem for total-body strength, squat mechanics, hinge strength, and simple force transfer.

categories.title: Rotation, Anti-Rotation, Core, and Power Transfer
categories.focus: Rotational and anti-rotational landmine drills that connect hips, trunk, shoulders, foot pivoting, and sport-like diagonal force production.

categories.title: Rows, Pulls, Hinges, and Handle-Grip Loading
categories.focus: Landmine pulling and hinge variations that load upper-back strength, lats, grip, posterior chain, trunk stiffness, and unilateral pulling control.

categories.title: Ball-Grip and Handle-Grip Specialty Variations
categories.focus: Attachment-based landmine variations using ball grips, neutral handles, V-handles, and handle grips to change wrist position, grip demand, loading angle, and shoulder comfort.

cards.name: Half-Kneeling One-Arm Landmine Press
cards.category: 01-10 - One-Handed Pressing, Stance, and Shoulder-Friendly Push Strength

gripProfile.gripCoachingRule: Grip should organize the shoulder and trunk; do not select an attachment that makes the wrist, elbow, or shoulder painful.

cards.cardSummary: Half-Kneeling One-Arm Landmine Press is a landmine exercise for shoulder-friendly unilateral press with trunk and pelvis control. Use it when the goal is quality force, posture, and repeatable bar path rather than fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Place after Prepare & Access and any needed Movement Intelligence. Output variants come before heavy strength or conditioning; Capacity and Resilience variants belong in the main strength or accessory block before late-session fatigue work.
cards.description: Perform Half-Kneeling One-Arm Landmine Press as a landmine-based push pattern. The athlete uses the fixed angled bar path to create useful load while maintaining bracing, breathing, grip ownership, and a repeatable finish position.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: upper_body_push_strength. Coach the athlete to earn load through setup, grip, stance, trunk pressure, and bar-path repeatability before chasing heavier plates or faster reps.
cards.athleteLanguage: Own the position first, move the bar second, and stop before the shape changes.
cards.selectionReason: shoulder-friendly unilateral press with trunk and pelvis control
cards.whyItWorks: The landmine gives Half-Kneeling One-Arm Landmine Press a constrained arc, reducing some degrees of freedom while still demanding bracing, grip, shoulder control, and lower-body contribution. This makes the drill easier to coach than many free-bar alternatives while still creating a meaningful strength or power problem.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in capacity because the main training stress is shoulder-friendly unilateral press with trunk and pelvis control. Keep the phase honest: use low volume and full recovery for Output, progressive load for Capacity, and calm control for Resilience.
cards.commonMisuse: Common mistakes are overloading the sleeve, rushing the eccentric, twisting through the low back instead of pivoting, letting the bar drift off its arc, and turning the drill into a conditioning station.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range, stance width, kneeling vs standing position, tempo, attachment choice, rep speed, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep the same bar path and breathing under every rep.

gripProfile.wristRule: neutral, pain-free wrist; choose ball or handle only when it improves control.

coachingExecution.movementDescription: press the bar through the angled landmine path while the trunk stays stacked
setup: Anchor the bar in a secure landmine base or immovable corner that cannot slide.
Load the bar conservatively and make sure the sleeve path is clear.
Hold the sleeve or single handle with one hand; keep the wrist stacked and shoulder packed.
Stack ribs over pelvis, brace gently, and choose a range the shoulder can own.
executionSteps: Press through the landmine arc while keeping ribs down and shoulder moving freely.
Finish with the arm long and the body stacked, then return under control.
Continue only while every rep matches the first clean rep.
coachCues: Press through the sleeve, not around it.
commonFaults: Using too much load before owning the bar path.
Letting the bar drift or bounce at the end range.
Holding breath so hard that posture or pressure changes.
Pressing only with the arm while the trunk leaks.
Rib flare at lockout.

dosage.qualityCap: Stop the set when bar speed, posture, grip, breathing, or joint position changes.

scaling.youthBeginner: Teach Half-Kneeling One-Arm Landmine Press with lightest practical landmine setup, reduced range, slow setup, and 3-5 clean reps. Use the simplest sleeve grip before adding specialty attachments.
scaling.youthIntermediate: Add small load jumps or slightly longer range only after both sides match and breathing stays calm.
scaling.teen: Use moderate load, crisp reps, and 1-3 reps in reserve; supervise closely on rotational and Output variations.
scaling.adultBeginner: Start with a stance, range, and attachment that makes the movement pain-free and repeatable before progressing load.
scaling.adultAdvanced: Progress load, tempo contrast, pauses, unilateral stance, or speed intent while preserving the same landmine arc.
scaling.olderAdult: Use a higher handle position, smaller range, slower tempo, and more rest; prioritize joint comfort and easy exits.
scaling.pregnancyPostpartum: Use symptom-free ranges, avoid prolonged breath-holding, and substitute supported or half-kneeling versions if pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, or pain appears.

readinessChecks: Landmine anchor is secure and the bar path is clear of athletes, walls, and loose plates.
Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain, dizziness, or uncontrolled breath-holding.
Athlete can brace, breathe, and stop the bar immediately if posture, grip, or joint position changes.
Load, range, stance, and speed are low enough that the first set looks repeatable.
contraindications: Sharp shoulder, elbow, wrist, back, hip, or knee pain during the pattern.
No safe landmine anchor or no clear lane for the bar arc.
Athlete cannot pivot, brace, or keep ribs/pelvis organized at the chosen range.
Grip attachment slips, rotates unpredictably, or cannot be secured.
commonSubstitutions: Half-kneeling landmine press
Tall-kneeling cable press or chop
Medicine ball scoop toss for unloaded power transfer
Dumbbell or cable version with a smaller range

cards.cardSummary: One-Arm Landmine Split Jerk is a landmine exercise for catch-under power press for advanced athletes with strong landing control. Use it when the goal is quality force, posture, and repeatable bar path rather than fatigue chasing.

cards.description: Perform One-Arm Landmine Split Jerk as a landmine-based push pattern. The athlete uses the fixed angled bar path to create useful load while maintaining bracing, breathing, grip ownership, and a repeatable finish position.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: jump_throw_explosive_power. Coach the athlete to earn load through setup, grip, stance, trunk pressure, and bar-path repeatability before chasing heavier plates or faster reps.
cards.athleteLanguage: Own the position first, move the bar second, and stop before the shape changes.
cards.selectionReason: catch-under power press for advanced athletes with strong landing control

cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in output because the main training stress is catch-under power press for advanced athletes with strong landing control. Keep the phase honest: use low volume and full recovery for Output, progressive load for Capacity, and calm control for Resilience.
cards.commonMisuse: Common mistakes are overloading the sleeve, rushing the eccentric, twisting through the low back instead of pivoting, letting the bar drift off its arc, and turning the drill into a conditioning station.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range, stance width, kneeling vs standing position, tempo, attachment choice, rep speed, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep the same bar path and breathing under every rep.

gripProfile.wristRule: neutral, pain-free wrist; choose ball or handle only when it improves control.

coachingExecution.movementDescription: press the bar through the angled landmine path while the trunk stays stacked
setup: Anchor the bar in a secure landmine base or immovable corner that cannot slide.
Load the bar conservatively and make sure the sleeve path is clear.
Hold the sleeve or single handle with one hand; keep the wrist stacked and shoulder packed.
Stack ribs over pelvis, brace gently, and choose a range the shoulder can own.
executionSteps: Press through the landmine arc while keeping ribs down and shoulder moving freely.
Finish with the arm long and the body stacked, then return under control.
Reset fully between reps so each rep is fast, crisp, and technically repeatable.
coachCues: Press through the sleeve, not around it.
commonFaults: Using too much load before owning the bar path.
Letting the bar drift or bounce at the end range.
Holding breath so hard that posture or pressure changes.
Pressing only with the arm while the trunk leaks.
Rib flare at lockout.

dosage.qualityCap: Stop the set when bar speed, posture, grip, breathing, or joint position changes.

scaling.youthBeginner: Teach One-Arm Landmine Split Jerk with empty bar or very light load with full reset, reduced range, slow setup, and 3-5 clean reps. Use the simplest sleeve grip before adding specialty attachments.
scaling.youthIntermediate: Add small load jumps or slightly longer range only after both sides match and breathing stays calm.
scaling.teen: Use moderate load, crisp reps, and 1-3 reps in reserve; supervise closely on rotational and Output variations.
scaling.adultBeginner: Start with a stance, range, and attachment that makes the movement pain-free and repeatable before progressing load.
scaling.adultAdvanced: Progress load, tempo contrast, pauses, unilateral stance, or speed intent while preserving the same landmine arc.
scaling.olderAdult: Use a higher handle position, smaller range, slower tempo, and more rest; prioritize joint comfort and easy exits.
scaling.pregnancyPostpartum: Use symptom-free ranges, avoid prolonged breath-holding, and substitute supported or half-kneeling versions if pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, or pain appears.

contraindications: Sharp shoulder, elbow, wrist, back, hip, or knee pain during the pattern.

Athlete cannot pivot, brace, or keep ribs/pelvis organized at the chosen range.

coachingExecution.movementDescription: step or split, load the front leg, then finish through a controlled landmine press
setup: Anchor the bar in a secure landmine base or immovable corner that cannot slide.

executionSteps: Load the working leg, keep the pelvis level, and avoid collapsing into the front hip or knee.

cards.description: Perform Landmine Clean to Press as a landmine-based hinge push pattern. The athlete uses the fixed angled bar path to create useful load while maintaining bracing, breathing, grip ownership, and a repeatable finish position.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: jump_throw_explosive_power. Coach the athlete to earn load through setup, grip, stance, trunk pressure, and bar-path repeatability before chasing heavier plates or faster reps.
cards.athleteLanguage: Own the position first, move the bar second, and stop before the shape changes.
cards.selectionReason: hinge-to-press power pattern with a simpler catch than a free barbell clean
cards.whyItWorks: The landmine gives Landmine Clean to Press a constrained arc, reducing some degrees of freedom while still demanding bracing, grip, shoulder control, and lower-body contribution. This makes the drill easier to coach than many free-bar alternatives while still creating a meaningful strength or power problem.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in output because the main training stress is hinge-to-press power pattern with a simpler catch than a free barbell clean. Keep the phase honest: use low volume and full recovery for Output, progressive load for Capacity, and calm control for Resilience.
cards.commonMisuse: Common mistakes are overloading the sleeve, rushing the eccentric, twisting through the low back instead of pivoting, letting the bar drift off its arc, and turning the drill into a conditioning station.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range, stance width, kneeling vs standing position, tempo, attachment choice, rep speed, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep the same bar path and breathing under every rep.

gripProfile.wristRule: neutral, pain-free wrist; choose ball or handle only when it improves control.

coachingExecution.movementDescription: hinge to load the hips, then transfer force into a clean press through the landmine arc
setup: Anchor the bar in a secure landmine base or immovable corner that cannot slide.
Load the bar conservatively and make sure the sleeve path is clear.
Hold the sleeve or single handle with one hand; keep the wrist stacked and shoulder packed.
Hinge to the working height while keeping the spine long and the bar close enough to control.
executionSteps: Push hips back, keep lats and trunk engaged, and stop before spinal position changes.
Extend through the hips rather than pulling the bar with the low back.
Press through the landmine arc while keeping ribs down and shoulder moving freely.
Finish with the arm long and the body stacked, then return under control.
Reset fully between reps so each rep is fast, crisp, and technically repeatable.
coachCues: Press through the sleeve, not around it.
commonFaults: Using too much load before owning the bar path.
Letting the bar drift or bounce at the end range.
Holding breath so hard that posture or pressure changes.
Pressing only with the arm while the trunk leaks.
Rib flare at lockout.

dosage.qualityCap: Stop the set when bar speed, posture, grip, breathing, or joint position changes.

scaling.youthBeginner: Teach Landmine Clean to Press with empty bar or very light load with full reset, reduced range, slow setup, and 3-5 clean reps. Use the simplest sleeve grip before adding specialty attachments.
scaling.youthIntermediate: Add small load jumps or slightly longer range only after both sides match and breathing stays calm.
scaling.teen: Use moderate load, crisp reps, and 1-3 reps in reserve; supervise closely on rotational and Output variations.
scaling.adultBeginner: Start with a stance, range, and attachment that makes the movement pain-free and repeatable before progressing load.
scaling.adultAdvanced: Progress load, tempo contrast, pauses, unilateral stance, or speed intent while preserving the same landmine arc.
scaling.olderAdult: Use a higher handle position, smaller range, slower tempo, and more rest; prioritize joint comfort and easy exits.
scaling.pregnancyPostpartum: Use symptom-free ranges, avoid prolonged breath-holding, and substitute supported or half-kneeling versions if pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, or pain appears.

readinessChecks: Landmine anchor is secure and the bar path is clear of athletes, walls, and loose plates.
Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain, dizziness, or uncontrolled breath-holding.
Athlete can brace, breathe, and stop the bar immediately if posture, grip, or joint position changes.
Load, range, stance, and speed are low enough that the first set looks repeatable.
contraindications: Sharp shoulder, elbow, wrist, back, hip, or knee pain during the pattern.
No safe landmine anchor or no clear lane for the bar arc.
Athlete cannot pivot, brace, or keep ribs/pelvis organized at the chosen range.
Grip attachment slips, rotates unpredictably, or cannot be secured.
commonSubstitutions: Half-kneeling landmine press
Tall-kneeling cable press or chop
Medicine ball scoop toss for unloaded power transfer
Dumbbell or cable version with a smaller range

cards.selectionReason: static ball-grip hold that combines anti-rotation with hand, wrist, and shoulder stability

coachingExecution.movementDescription: hold the landmine load in a fixed position while resisting rotation, extension, and grip collapse
setup: Anchor the bar in a secure landmine base or immovable corner that cannot slide.
Load the bar conservatively and make sure the sleeve path is clear.
Attach the ball grip securely and test that it does not slip before the work set.
Stack ribs over pelvis, brace gently, and choose a range the shoulder can own.
executionSteps: Hold the bar in the target position and breathe without losing the rib-pelvis stack.
End the rep before the bar drifts, grip collapses, or the body twists.
Continue only while every rep matches the first clean rep.
commonFaults: Using too much load before owning the bar path.
Letting the bar drift or bounce at the end range.
Holding breath so hard that posture or pressure changes.
Choosing an attachment that irritates the wrist or slips under load.

dosage.qualityCap: Stop the set when bar speed, posture, grip, breathing, or joint position changes.

scaling.youthBeginner: Teach Landmine Ball-Grip Anti-Rotation Hold with lightest practical landmine setup, reduced range, slow setup, and 3-5 clean reps. Use ball grip only after the athlete can keep wrist, elbow, and shoulder stacked with a standard grip.
scaling.youthIntermediate: Add small load jumps or slightly longer range only after both sides match and breathing stays calm.
scaling.teen: Use moderate load, crisp reps, and 1-3 reps in reserve; supervise closely on rotational and Output variations.
scaling.adultBeginner: Start with a stance, range, and attachment that makes the movement pain-free and repeatable before progressing load.
scaling.adultAdvanced: Progress load, tempo contrast, pauses, unilateral stance, or speed intent while preserving the same landmine arc.
scaling.olderAdult: Use a higher handle position, smaller range, slower tempo, and more rest; prioritize joint comfort and easy exits.
scaling.pregnancyPostpartum: Use symptom-free ranges, avoid prolonged breath-holding, and substitute supported or half-kneeling versions if pressure, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, or pain appears.

executionSteps: Load the working leg, keep the pelvis level, and avoid collapsing into the front hip or knee.
Drive back to the start position while the bar follows the same arc each rep.
Continue only while every rep matches the first clean rep.
commonFaults: Using too much load before owning the bar path.

cards.category: 41-50 - Ball-Grip and Handle-Grip Specialty Variations

gripProfile.gripCoachingRule: Grip should organize the shoulder and trunk; do not select an attachment that makes the wrist, elbow, or shoulder painful.

cards.cardSummary: Landmine Handle-Grip Rotational Row is a landmine exercise for rotational row with handle grip for lats, trunk, hip turn, and grip strength. Use it when the goal is quality force, posture, and repeatable bar path rather than fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Place after Prepare & Access and any needed Movement Intelligence. Output variants come before heavy strength or conditioning; Capacity and Resilience variants belong in the main strength or accessory block before late-session fatigue work.
cards.description: Perform Landmine Handle-Grip Rotational Row as a landmine-based pull rotate pattern. The athlete uses the fixed angled bar path to create useful load while maintaining bracing, breathing, grip ownership, and a repeatable finish position.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: upper_body_pull_strength. Coach the athlete to earn load through setup, grip, stance, trunk pressure, and bar-path repeatability before chasing heavier plates or faster reps.
cards.athleteLanguage: Own the position first, move the bar second, and stop before the shape changes.
cards.selectionReason: rotational row with handle grip for lats, trunk, hip turn, and grip strength
cards.whyItWorks: The landmine gives Landmine Handle-Grip Rotational Row a constrained arc, reducing some degrees of freedom while still demanding bracing, grip, shoulder control, and lower-body contribution. This makes the drill easier to coach than many free-bar alternatives while still creating a meaningful strength or power problem.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in capacity because the main training stress is rotational row with handle grip for lats, trunk, hip turn, and grip strength. Keep the phase honest: use low volume and full recovery for Output, progressive load for Capacity, and calm control for Resilience.
cards.commonMisuse: Common mistakes are overloading the sleeve, rushing the eccentric, twisting through the low back instead of pivoting, letting the bar drift off its arc, and turning the drill into a conditioning station.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range, stance width, kneeling vs standing position, tempo, attachment choice, rep speed, and rest. Progress only when the athlete can keep the same bar path and breathing under every rep.

gripProfile.wristRule: neutral, pain-free wrist; choose ball or handle only when it improves control.

coachingExecution.movementDescription: row the landmine while allowing controlled hip rotation without losing trunk stiffness
setup: Anchor the bar in a secure landmine base or immovable corner that cannot slide.
Load the bar conservatively and make sure the sleeve path is clear.
Attach the handle, V-handle, or neutral grip securely and confirm it matches the intended wrist position.
Hinge to the working height while keeping the spine long and the bar close enough to control.
executionSteps: Pull the sleeve or handle toward the target line while the shoulder blade moves cleanly.
Lower under control until the arm is long but the shoulder is not dumped forward.
Initiate rotation from the hips, pivot the feet, and let the trunk and press follow.
Control the return path instead of letting the bar pull the spine or shoulder.
Continue only while every rep matches the first clean rep.
coachCues: Row with the back, not the neck.
Long arm down, strong shoulder back.
Rotate through the hips, not the low back.
commonFaults: Using too much load before owning the bar path.
Letting the bar drift or bounce at the end range.
Holding breath so hard that posture or pressure changes.
Yanking with the neck and upper trap.
Losing hinge position to finish the row.
Twisting through the low back instead of pivoting.
Locking the feet while forcing rotation.
Choosing an attachment that irritates the wrist or slips under load.
